panel.tagline: Train a model, understand every choice.

onboarding.title: Let's get to know you
onboarding.subtitle: Three questions to adapt IBIS-X to your profile — especially the level of detail of AI explanations.
onboarding.educationHelp: This helps us calibrate the vocabulary we use.
onboarding.familiarityTitle: How comfortable are you with AI and Machine Learning?
onboarding.familiarityHelp: 1 = complete beginner, 5 = very comfortable. You can change this later in your profile.

audiencePreview.novice: Your explanations will be simple, with everyday analogies.
audiencePreview.intermediate: Your explanations will be structured and decision-oriented.
audiencePreview.expert: Your explanations will use exact technical terminology.

onboarding.ageReassurance: Used only to adjust the tone of explanations — never shared.

challenges.title: Challenges
challenges.subtitle: Practise on real cases — from the question to the explanation.
challenges.intro: Each challenge is a real data investigation: you go through the actual pipeline and leave with a real model and its explanation.

levelHints.novice: On rails: the AI guides you at every step.
levelHints.debutant: Copilot: you make the key choices, the AI stays available.
levelHints.confirme: On your own: you drive, we check the result.

challenges.debriefRealResult: This result is real: trained on real data, measured, never simulated.
challenges.debriefRemaining: One objective left to close the investigation: {objective}.
challenges.downloadHint: Your model can be downloaded at the top of the page — a real, reusable artefact.
challenges.debriefMetric: A real model, trained on real data: {metric}.
challenges.debriefScoreHint: This score runs from 0 to 1: the closer to 1, the better the model sees. No need to be an expert — what matters is understanding WHY it decides, in the next step.
challenges.debriefExplainCta: One last, most enlightening step: understanding WHY. Open the "Explainability" tab just below and click "Generate explanation" — the AI will show you, in plain words, which information weighed most on the predictions.
challenges.debriefExplainDone: You generated the explanation: no more black box — you understand what the model relies on. That's explainable AI.

debriefPont.novice: You just watched a full ML pipeline succeed, end to end.
debriefPont.debutant: The most decisive factor is actionable — the kind of conclusion a researcher publishes.
debriefPont.confirme: Real metrics, a downloadable model: this is exactly the gesture of research work.

challenges.resolveError: Could not open this challenge's dataset right now.

titanic-1912.title: 1912: who survived, and why?
titanic-1912.brief: April 1912 — the Titanic sinks in the North Atlantic. Over a century later, its passenger list is still one of the most studied datasets in the world. From 891 real passengers (class, sex, age, fare…), can a model predict who survived?
titanic-1912.stakes: Behind the accuracy lies a social question: did a passenger's fate depend first on their class and their sex?
titanic-1912.reward: You'll leave with your first trained model — and an explanation of what mattered most.

coach.at_dataset: Click "Use in a project" to start from this dataset.
coach.at_project: Name your project, then click "Create and launch training".
coach.at_wizard: Apply the AI recommendation at each step, then launch training.
coach.at_results: Open the "Explainability" tab (below) and click "Generate explanation": the AI will tell you, in plain words, what mattered.

penguins-antarctique.title: Fieldwork in Antarctica: which species?
penguins-antarctique.tagline: Identify a penguin with a tape measure and a scale.
penguins-antarctique.brief: Palmer Station, Antarctica. A field biologist jots down a few measurements — bill length, mass, flipper — without always getting a close look at the bird. Are those numbers enough to tell three penguin species apart?
penguins-antarctique.stakes: A good classifier means faster, reproducible wildlife surveys in the field.
penguins-antarctique.reward: You'll leave with a 3-class classifier and a confusion matrix showing where it hesitates.

coach.at_dataset: Click "Use in a project" to start from this dataset.
coach.at_project: Name your project, then click "Create and launch training".
coach.at_wizard: Let the AI recommendation guide you at each step, then launch.
coach.at_results: Look at the confusion matrix, then open the "Explainability" tab and click "Generate explanation".

eleves-decrochage.title: Summer 2026: who's at risk of failing?
eleves-decrochage.tagline: Help a teacher spot struggling students early.
eleves-decrochage.brief: Back to school, 2026. A teaching team has real data on 395 students (attendance, family context, mid-term grades…). Can we spot, early in the year, those at risk of failing — so we can support them better?
eleves-decrochage.stakes: The stakes here are human: a reliable signal means acting before it's too late, without stigmatising anyone.
eleves-decrochage.reward: You'll leave with a model, and the explanation of the single most decisive factor.

depistage-diabete.title: Catch diabetes before it speaks
depistage-diabete.tagline: An early warning signal for a clinic.
depistage-diabete.brief: A health centre wants an early-warning tool for diabetes from routine clinical measurements (glucose, BMI, age, history…). 768 real patients. Where does the risk hide?
depistage-diabete.stakes: In healthcare, a prediction isn't enough: you must be able to explain WHY, case by case.
depistage-diabete.reward: You'll leave with a model, and a local explanation on a specific case.

noter-un-vin.title: Rate a wine without tasting it
noter-un-vin.brief: A winery wants to score its reds without a tasting panel, from physico-chemical analysis alone (acidity, sugar, alcohol…). 1,599 real wines rated 0 to 10. Is chemistry enough?
noter-un-vin.stakes: This is a regression problem: you predict a continuous score, not a label — and you measure the real error.
noter-un-vin.reward: You'll leave with a regression model, its R²/MAE metrics, and a downloadable model.

iris-hello-world.title: Data science's hello world
iris-hello-world.tagline: Three irises, four measurements — the 1936 classic.
iris-hello-world.brief: 1936. Botanist Edgar Anderson measures irises; statistician Ronald Fisher turns them into the most famous dataset in history. Four measurements, three species. Your turn to repeat the founding gesture — on your own.
iris-hello-world.stakes: The absolute benchmark: perfect for comparing two algorithms and finding your bearings.
iris-hello-world.reward: You'll leave with a near-perfect classifier and a basis for comparing models.

equite-revenus.title: Income: a matter of gender?
equite-revenus.tagline: Predict who earns over $50k — then hunt the bias.
equite-revenus.brief: 48,000 Americans, 1994 census. Train a model to predict who earns more than $50k a year. Then ask the uncomfortable question: which variables does it truly rely on to decide — education and hours worked, or sex and origin? Run the audit yourself, on your own.
equite-revenus.stakes: This is the heart of algorithmic fairness: a high-performing model can encode — and amplify — the inequalities in its data.
equite-revenus.reward: You'll leave with a real model, and the XAI explanation that reveals which variables it actually relies on.

equite-credit.title: A loan denied… because of what?
equite-credit.brief: 1,000 German credit applications. Build a model that tells good from bad borrowers, then put on your auditor's hat: what truly drives the decision — repayment history, or the applicant's age and status? Run the investigation on your own.
equite-credit.stakes: A credit score decides real lives. Understanding its levers means being able to challenge it — the very subject of responsible-AI research.
equite-credit.reward: You'll leave with a real model, and an explanation that lays its decision criteria bare.

card.ethicalHint: Ethical score: an assessment of the dataset's ethical quality (bias, transparency, anonymization, consent…). The higher it is, the better the dataset meets these criteria.

detail.previewSampled: Random sample of {rows} rows (seed {seed}) out of {total} — stable across reloads.

scoring.empty: No dataset to score — adjust the catalog filters.
scoring.activeCriteria: {count, plural, =0 {No active criterion} one {# active criterion} other {# active criteria}}
scoring.scoreSelectionCount: {count, plural, one {Score this dataset} other {Score these # datasets}}

projects.results: {count, plural, =0 {No project} one {# project} other {# projects}}

card.criteria: {count, plural, =0 {no criteria} one {# criterion} other {# criteria}}

empty.title: No project yet
empty.body: Create your first project: define your criteria and priorities, IBIS-X will recommend the most suitable datasets.

form.name: Project name
form.criteriaHint: Same filters as the catalog: only matching datasets will be recommended. No criteria = whole catalog.
form.previewCount: {count, plural, =0 {No dataset matches} one {# dataset matches} other {# datasets match}}
form.createSubtitle: Three steps: details, selection criteria, weights — your recommendations refine live in the preview.
form.editSubtitle: Adjust the criteria or the weights: recommendations recompute live in the preview.
form.directSubtitle: You already know which dataset to use — name your project, then start training right away.
form.selectedDatasetHint: No need for selection criteria or weightings here — your dataset is already chosen.

detail.deleteConfirmBody: Associated experiments will also be deleted. This action is permanent.
detail.experimentsEmptyBody: The guided training pipeline (9 steps) arrives in the next milestone: you will launch trainings from this project and compare them here.
detail.noCriteria: No criteria: the whole catalog is considered.
detail.recommendationsHint: These datasets match your criteria, ranked by your weights. Open the wizard on the one that suits you to start a training run.

wizard.title: Guided training
wizard.missingContext: The wizard opens from a project: pick a recommended dataset then “Start a training”.
wizard.draftResumed: Draft resumed — continue where you left off.

step1.understandBody: A dataset is a table, like a spreadsheet: each row is an example (a student, a flower, a passenger…) and each column a measured piece of information. The model learns regularities in these rows to predict a target column.
step1.qualityHint: 100 − penalties (missing values, outliers) — computed on the full file.

step2.targetLabel: Target column (what the model must predict)
step2.classificationHint: Predict a category (e.g. setosa / versicolor / virginica).
step2.regressionHint: Predict a number (e.g. a grade from 0 to 20).
step2.reasonCategorical: Target “{column}” holds {count} distinct text/category values: classification fits.
step2.reasonNumeric: Target “{column}” is continuous numeric ({count} distinct values): regression fits.
step2.blockBody: Target “{column}” is categorical: regression is impossible. Choose classification, or a numeric target.
step2.understandBody: Classification = predict a label among categories. Regression = predict a continuous numeric value. It depends on the target column.
step2.classificationFor: Predict the category of “{column}”: each row will be assigned to one of the existing groups.
step2.regressionFor: Predict the numeric value of “{column}”: the model will estimate a number for each row.
step2.notAppropriateBody: Not appropriate: the column “{column}” represents categories, not continuous values.
step2.notApplicable: Not applicable — the variable is not continuous.
step2.aiAvailableTarget: The AI can suggest what to predict
step2.suggestTargetIntro: To get you started, I analysed your columns.
step2.noSuggestionBody: Pick the column the model should predict below. As soon as it's selected, I'll advise the right task type.

step3.intro: Columns to clean: choose a strategy for every column with missing values. What you configure here is REALLY applied during training.
step3.cleanBody: Your data is excellent — no unnecessary treatment will be applied (over-processing degrades models).
step3.blockOver30: These columns exceed 30% missing and require an explicit strategy: {columns}
step3.targetMissing: Target “{column}” has {count} missing values: those rows will be dropped at training (shown in the recap).

step4.stratified: Automatic stratification in classification: each class keeps the same proportion in train and test.
step4.seed: random_state = 42 (fixed — reproducibility guaranteed: two identical runs give identical results)
step4.understandBody: The model learns on train then is evaluated on test — data it has NEVER seen. It is the only honest way to measure generalization.

step5.scalingHint: E.g. [10, 20, 30] → [−1, 0, +1]: all features speak the same scale “language”.
step5.robustReco: Outliers were detected at step 3: the robust method is recommended.
step5.applied: This configuration is really transmitted and applied during training.
step5.aiReco: Based on your data, keep Standard scaling (μ=0, σ=1): it brings every numeric feature to the same scale. For categorical features, One-hot encoding creates one column per value without imposing an order. These are the defaults below.
step5.aiRecoOutliers: Outliers were detected at step 3: prefer Robust scaling, which resists those extremes, over Standard. For categorical features, One-hot encoding is still recommended (one column per value, no imposed order).

descriptions.decision_tree: A readable series of if/then questions. 100% interpretable, fast, ideal to understand decisions — may lack accuracy on complex data.
descriptions.random_forest: Hundreds of trees voting. Robust and accurate (bonus OOB score) — slightly less readable than a single tree.

step6.reasonSmall: Compact dataset ({rows} rows): a decision tree stays readable and sufficient.
step6.reasonLarge: {rows} rows and {cols} features: the random forest will handle the complexity better.

hints.max_depth: Maximum depth: higher = more precise but overfitting risk.
hints.min_samples_split: Minimum samples to split a node: higher = simpler tree.
hints.min_samples_leaf: Minimum samples per leaf: higher = more robust to noise.
hints.n_estimators: Number of trees: more = more stable but slower.

step7.aiReco: The « Balanced » preset fits most cases — a good trade-off between accuracy and robustness. Switch to « Custom » to tune each setting yourself.

step8.recapHint: The full recipe for your training run — every setting you confirmed.
step8.processing: The AI is training your model…

errors.MAX_CONCURRENT_TRAININGS: You already have 3 trainings running — wait for one to finish.
errors.INSUFFICIENT_CREDITS: Not enough credits: ask an administrator for a top-up.

subtitles.1: Review your dataset before you begin.
subtitles.2: Choose what you want to predict and the type of problem.
subtitles.3: Decide, column by column, how to handle missing values.
subtitles.4: Hold out part of the data to evaluate the model honestly.
subtitles.5: Scaling and encoding — the last settings before the algorithm.
subtitles.6: Pick the algorithm that fits your data.
subtitles.7: Fine-tune the settings, or trust the presets.
subtitles.8: Review, confirm, launch — follow the training live.
subtitles.9: Your model is ready — on to the results.

wizard.aiAvailable: An AI recommendation is available for this step

experiments.xaiSoon: Explainability (SHAP/LIME, reliability KPIs, chat) arrives in the next milestone.

metricHints.f1_macro: Unweighted mean of per-class F1 — more representative in multiclass.
metricHints.roc_auc: Ability to separate classes (1 = perfect, 0.5 = random).
metricHints.mae: Mean absolute error, in target units.

charts.importanceHint: NATIVE model importance (Gini) — this is not SHAP.

table.selectHint: Select 2 to 8 completed experiments to compare them.

request.title: Request an explanation
request.typeGlobalHint: Which features matter most, in general, for this model.
request.typeLocalHint: Why the model made one specific prediction, for an example you pick.
request.typeLabel: What do you want to understand?
request.instanceIntro: Each row is a test example — data the model never saw during training. The “gap” shows how far off it was (Actual vs Predicted): biggest gaps first, as they're the most instructive cases.
request.pickInstancePrompt: Click a row to choose the example to explain.

methodHint.auto: The AI automatically picks the most reliable method for your model. Best if you're new to this.
methodHint.shap: SHAP splits the prediction across features in a mathematically exact way. For a rigorous explanation.
methodHint.lime: LIME approximates the model around your example with a simple model. Fast and intuitive, but approximate.

history.title: History
history.empty: No explanation generated for this experiment yet.

kpis.title: Explanation reliability
kpis.hint: Every indicator is really computed — if not computable, it is absent.
kpis.completenessHint: Efficiency axiom: Σ contributions + base = prediction (gap < 1%).
kpis.fidelityHint: R² of the local linear model approximating the model.
kpis.agreementHint: Rank correlation of top 10 between both methods.
kpis.parsimonyHint: Number of features covering 80% of total importance.

charts.importanceHint: The longer the bar, the more that feature weighs in the model's decisions.
charts.waterfallHint: Start from a base value (the average), then each feature pushes the prediction one way or the other.
charts.comparisonHint: Two methods, two ways to measure importance. Strong agreement between them = a more reliable explanation.

text.title: Explanation
text.subtitle: Written by the AI from the numbers above.

chat.title: Discuss this explanation
chat.remaining: {count, plural, =0 {Quota reached (5 questions)} one {# question left} other {# questions left}}
chat.expired: Session expired — start a new chat.

result.title: Explanation result
result.localFor: For test example #{index}, the model predicted:
result.localWhy: . Here's what tipped it this way.
result.globalSubtitle: The features that matter most, on average, across all predictions.

intro.title: Understand your model's decisions
intro.body: An explanation turns into plain language why the model predicts what it predicts: which information mattered, and in which direction. Useful to trust it — and to learn how it reasons.
intro.globalBody: Which features matter most, on average, across all predictions.
intro.localBody: Why the model answered what it did, for a given example.
intro.cta: Set up your request, then hit “Generate explanation”.

generating.title: The AI is generating your explanation…
generating.subtitle: Analysing the model and putting it into words. Just a few seconds.

steps.read: Reading the model and test data

landing.subtitle: Pick a dataset on technical AND ethical criteria, train a model through 9 guided steps, then understand its decisions with explainability.
landing.phase1Body: A documented catalog, a weighted 12-criteria score and a comparative heatmap to choose with confidence.
landing.phase2Body: 9 pedagogical steps, methodological guardrails, guaranteed reproducibility (random_state=42).
landing.phase3Body: SHAP and LIME with computed reliability indicators, profile-adapted explanation and chat.
landing.research: Born from a research project — M2 MIAGE, Université Paris 1 Panthéon-Sorbonne.

preview.caption: Product preview — a faithful reconstruction in real components, not a screenshot.

journey.title: The journey, in four steps
journey.subtitle: The same thread guides you through every stage of the application.
journey.projectBody: Describe your goal: the application then suggests datasets weighted against your priorities.

ethics.title: How we assess data
ethics.subtitle: Twelve technical and ethical criteria, visible before you pick a dataset.

honesty.title: An AI that explains its limits

fallback.title: Fallback, made explicit
fallback.body: If SHAP isn't available for your model, the application switches to LIME and says so plainly — never a disguised result.

reproducibility.title: Reproducible
reproducibility.body: Every training run fixes the random seed (random_state=42): same data, same result.

kpis.title: Measured reliability
kpis.body: SHAP completeness, stability, LIME fidelity: computed indicators, not promises.

experimentsPage.empty: No experiment. Start a training from a project.

users.subtitle: Roles, activation, credits and account deletion.
users.deleteBody: Projects, experiments and explanations of {email} will be permanently deleted.
users.lastAdmin: Not allowed: this is the last active administrator.

datasets.title: Datasets (administration)
datasets.subtitle: Every dataset in the catalog, including system ones.
datasets.deleteBody: “{name}” and its files will be deleted. Linked experiments will lose their source.

templates.title: Ethical templates
templates.subtitle: Per-domain defaults applied at import when a criterion is unset (the “default” domain is the fallback).
templates.empty: No template yet — create the first one above.
templates.deleteBody: Future imports for this domain will no longer inherit any default.

jobs.title: Jobs supervision
jobs.subtitle: Trainings, explanations and background tasks — jobs table fed by the worker.
jobs.noWorkers: No worker detected — trainings will stay pending.

layout.subtitle: Back office: accounts, catalogue, ethics and job supervision.

switcher.label: Lens
switcher.learn: Switch lenses to learn to read the same result differently.

reading.heading: Through the eyes of {name}
reading.tagline: Same real numbers — only the reading angle changes.
reading.noSensitive: No obviously sensitive variable detected by its name — but proxies may remain (e.g. ZIP code ↔ origin).
reading.sensitiveList: The model relies on potentially protected variables: {list}.

profile.title: My discipline
profile.description: Choose the lens applied to your results by default. You can always switch, and browse the other lenses to learn.

economist.name: the economist
economist.tagline: What the variables say — and what they don't.
economist.topFeature: The variable most associated with the target is “{feature}” (importance {percent}%).
economist.caveat: This is an association, not a causal effect — and an importance, not a coefficient with a confidence interval. A quantified, tested effect would require an identification design (OLS, DiD, instrumental variable).

jurist.name: the jurist
jurist.tagline: What the model may — or may not — use.
jurist.automated: An automated decision based on these variables falls under the right to explanation (GDPR, art. 22) and AI Act obligations.
jurist.caveat: A high-performing model can still be discriminatory. Performance is not lawfulness: fairness across groups must be audited.

politist.name: the political scientist
politist.tagline: Who are we talking about, and can we generalize?
politist.sampleClassification: Result obtained on a sample split into {count} groups ({names}).
politist.sampleGeneric: Result obtained on a data sample.
politist.representativeness: The conclusion only holds if this sample represents the target population.
politist.caveat: Without survey weights or a sampling design (absent here), generalizing to the population must stay cautious.

sociologist.name: the sociologist
sociologist.tagline: What social relations lie behind the numbers?
sociologist.mechanism: Behind “{feature}”, what social mechanisms? The model captures a regularity, not an explanation.
sociologist.structure: Socio-demographic variables encode social positions, never individual fates.
sociologist.caveat: Risk of naturalizing inequalities: correlating is not explaining the social mechanism that produces the result.

historian.name: the historian
historian.tagline: Where does this data come from, and what does it silence?
historian.provenance: This data was produced at a specific time and in a specific context, to be questioned before any conclusion.
historian.silence: A model aggregates: it erases the singular case, the exception and the context the qualitative gaze seeks.
historian.caveat: Data is historically situated; numbers never speak for themselves.

ethicist.name: the AI ethicist
ethicist.tagline: Can we — and should we — use this model?
ethicist.honesty: Is the explanation honest about what it knows? IBIS-X explicitly flags a no-AI fallback rather than inventing.
ethicist.purpose: Purpose and consent: was this data collected for this specific use?
ethicist.caveat: The real question goes beyond legality: is this model fair and acceptable?

causal.title: Association, not causation
causal.body: These variables are correlated with the target: the model uses them to predict. That does not prove they cause it. Establishing a causal effect requires an identification design (experiment, difference-in-differences, instrumental variable).
